pageContent:
[From the EEOC / Title VII / ADA / ADEA — covers employment discrimination, harassment, hostile work environment, wrongful termination, and reasonable accommodations.]

# EEOC - Leyes contra la Discriminación en el Empleo (2026)

La Comisión para la Igualdad de Oportunidades en el Empleo (EEOC) administra y hace cumplir las leyes de derechos civiles.

metadata.context: From the EEOC / Title VII / ADA / ADEA — covers employment discrimination, harassment, hostile work environment, wrongful termination, and reasonable accommodations.

pageContent:
[From the EEOC / Title VII / ADA / ADEA — covers employment discrimination, harassment, hostile work environment, wrongful termination, and reasonable accommodations.]

## 1. Tipos de Discriminación Ilegal
Es ilegal tratar a un empleado de manera desfavorable en función de:
- **Raza y Color:** Incluye diferencias en trato, pago, despidos o asensos por raza o tez de piel.
- **Religión:** Incluye la omisión a las adaptaciones razonables de las prácticas religiosas del empleado.
- **Sexo (Incluye Orientación Sexual e Identidad de Género):** Prohibido por el Title VII de la Ley de Derechos Civiles y rectificado en los fallos recientes de la Corte Suprema (Bostock vs. Clayton County). Incluye protección frente al acoso y al embarazo.
- **Nacionalidad de Origen:** Prohíbe, entre otros, exigir "inglés fluido" si no es fundamental y estrictamente necesario para la labor. Todo inmigrante documentado está protegido íntegramente de represalias.
- **Edad:** Aplica (ADEA) para toda persona mayor de 40 años que sea "reemplazada" injustificadamente por empleados más jóvenes o presionada a la pre-jubilación.

metadata.context: From the EEOC / Title VII / ADA / ADEA — covers employment discrimination, harassment, hostile work environment, wrongful termination, and reasonable accommodations.

pageContent:
[From the EEOC / Title VII / ADA / ADEA — covers employment discrimination, harassment, hostile work environment, wrongful termination, and reasonable accommodations.]

- **Edad:** Aplica (ADEA) para toda persona mayor de 40 años que sea "reemplazada" injustificadamente por empleados más jóvenes o presionada a la pre-jubilación.
- **Discapacidad (ADA):** El empleador debe hacer "acomodos razonables" a empleados calificados con discapacidades reconocibles a menos de que cause una Dificultad Excesiva a la empresa. En 2026, esto incluye normativas más explícitas sobre la viabilidad del teletrabajo (home-office) como "acomodo razonable" en empresas privadas.
- **Información Genética y Represalias.**

metadata.context: From the EEOC / Title VII / ADA / ADEA — covers employment discrimination, harassment, hostile work environment, wrongful termination, and reasonable accommodations.

pageContent:
[From the EEOC / Title VII / ADA / ADEA — covers employment discrimination, harassment, hostile work environment, wrongful termination, and reasonable accommodations.]

## 2. Aplicabilidad
La mayoría de las leyes de la EEOC amparan a los empleados en empresas de 15 o más trabajadores (20 para casos de discriminación por edad).

## 3. Acoso (Harassment)
Comportamiento persistente no deseado que crea un ambiente de trabajo intimidante o abusivo basado en los rubros protegidos mencionados. Todo despido en este contexto es "Wrongful Termination", y si el ambiente empuja a una renuncia obligada, cuenta como "Despido Constructivo".

## 4. Programas de Diversidad, Equidad e Inclusión (DEI)
A partir de 2026, los programas DEI deben estar diseñados con cuidado extremo para que sus metodologías no representen "discriminación inversa" basadas en cuotas de raza o sexo exclusivas en contrataciones.

metadata.context: From the EEOC / Title VII / ADA / ADEA — covers employment discrimination, harassment, hostile work environment, wrongful termination, and reasonable accommodations.

pageContent:
[From the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) — covers minimum wage, overtime, tipped employees, exempt/non-exempt classification, child labor, and wage theft.]

# Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) - Guía Resumida (2026)

## 1. Salario Mínimo
- El salario mínimo federal sigue siendo **$7.25 por hora**, sin embargo, muchos estados tienen el suyo propio.
- **Regla:** Si un empleado está sujeto a las leyes de salario mínimo estatales y federales, tiene derecho a recibir la tasa más alta de las dos.
- **Trabajadores con Propina (Tipped Employees):** Los empleadores pueden tomar un crédito parcial contra la obligación de salario mínimo, siempre que las propinas del empleado sumadas aseguren que su compensación total alcance al menos el salario mínimo federal o estatal.

metadata.context: From the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) — covers minimum wage, overtime, tipped employees, exempt/non-exempt classification, child labor, and wage theft.

pageContent:
[From the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) — covers minimum wage, overtime, tipped employees, exempt/non-exempt classification, child labor, and wage theft.]

## 2. Horas Extras (Overtime)
- A los empleados no exentos amparados por la FLSA se les debe pagar el tiempo extra a una tasa no menor a **una vez y media (1.5x)** de su tarifa regular por todas las horas laboradas en exceso del límite de **40 horas en una semana laboral**.
- La FLSA no exige el pago de tiempo extra por trabajar fines de semana, días festivos o descansos normales, a menos que dichas horas sobrepasen las 40 en la semana.
- **Umbral Salarial de Exención (2026):** Para que un empleado sea "exento" (es decir, que no reciba overtime), debe cumplir con la prueba de deberes y superar el umbral federal de $684 semanales, aunque en estados como California ($1,352/semana) o NY ($1,275/semana) los límites son mucho mayores.

metadata.context: From the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) — covers minimum wage, overtime, tipped employees, exempt/non-exempt classification, child labor, and wage theft.

pageContent:
[From the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) — covers minimum wage, overtime, tipped employees, exempt/non-exempt classification, child labor, and wage theft.]

## 3. Trabajo Infantil (Child Labor)
- La FLSA prohíbe el empleo de menores en trabajos y condiciones perjudiciales para su salud y bienestar educativos.
- La edad mínima para la mayoría de los trabajos no agrícolas es **14 años**, con restricciones estrictas de horario para menores de 16.

## 4. Retención de Registros (Recordkeeping)
- Los empleadores están obligados a mantener registros de salarios, horas trabajadas y demás información laboral. No se requiere un formato particular; usar un reloj marcador no es obligatorio.

metadata.context: From the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) — covers minimum wage, overtime, tipped employees, exempt/non-exempt classification, child labor, and wage theft.

pageContent:
[From the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) — covers minimum wage, overtime, tipped employees, exempt/non-exempt classification, child labor, and wage theft.]

## 4. Retención de Registros (Recordkeeping)
- Los empleadores están obligados a mantener registros de salarios, horas trabajadas y demás información laboral. No se requiere un formato particular; usar un reloj marcador no es obligatorio.

## 5. Clasificación de Trabajadores (Contratista vs. Empleado)
- Para 2026, la prueba para determinar si un trabajador es contratista independiente bajo la FLSA se inclina fuertemente por dos factores principales: 
  1. La naturaleza y grado de control del trabajador sobre el trabajo.
  2. Su oportunidad de obtener ganancias o pérdidas basadas en su propia iniciativa e inversión.
- Llamarse a sí mismo "contratista independiente" no exime al jefe de responsabilidades de overtime y beneficios si el control lo ejerce verdaderamente la compañía.

metadata.context: From the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) — covers minimum wage, overtime, tipped employees, exempt/non-exempt classification, child labor, and wage theft.

pageContent:
[From the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) — covers minimum wage, overtime, tipped employees, exempt/non-exempt classification, child labor, and wage theft.]

> **Limitaciones de la FLSA:** Esta ley federal no regula el pago de vacaciones, indemnización por despido (severance), incapacidades pagadas (sick leave), ni los descansos y comidas, que dependen de las normativas estatales.

pageContent:
[From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.]

# US Immigration and Employment Law Summary (2026)

This document outlines the intersection of United States immigration law and employment rights as of 2026. It is designed to provide guidance to immigrant workers, regardless of their documentation status, and to summarize the laws governing employment-based visas.

## 1. Core Protections for Immigrant Workers (Regardless of Status)

In the United States, basic labor standards apply to **all workers**, whether they are documented, undocumented, or hold a temporary visa.

metadata.context: From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.

pageContent:
[From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.]

## 1. Core Protections for Immigrant Workers (Regardless of Status)

In the United States, basic labor standards apply to **all workers**, whether they are documented, undocumented, or hold a temporary visa. 

### Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) Protections
- **Minimum Wage and Overtime:** Undocumented workers are fully protected by the FLSA. They are legally entitled to the federal (or state, if higher) minimum wage and overtime pay (time-and-a-half for hours worked over 40 in a workweek).
- **Retaliation:** It is illegal for an employer to retaliate against a worker for complaining about unpaid wages. A common illegal retaliation tactic is threatening to call Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).

metadata.context: From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.

pageContent:
[From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.]

### Title VII and EEOC Protections
- **National Origin Discrimination:** Under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act, it is illegal to discriminate against an employee based on their national origin, birthplace, ancestry, culture, or linguistic characteristics (like an accent).
- **Language Policies:** Employers cannot enforce "English-only" rules unless they are an absolute business necessity for safe and efficient operation.
- **Project Firewall (2026):** The EEOC and DOL have partnered aggressively to root out disparate treatment of immigrant workers, especially in construction, hospitality, and agriculture.

metadata.context: From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.

pageContent:
[From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.]

### Workplace Occupational Safety and Health (OSHA)
- Immigrant workers have the exact same rights to a safe workplace under OSHA as U.S. citizens. Employers must provide necessary safety gear and training in a language the worker understands.
- Injured undocumented workers are generally eligible for Workers' Compensation limits, though rules vary by state regarding vocational rehabilitation.

## 2. Visas for Victims of Workplace Crimes or Trafficking

Workers who face severe abuse, trafficking, or crimes at the workplace may be eligible for specific visa protections that grant legal status and work authorization.

### U Visas (Victims of Crime)
- Available to immigrants who have suffered substantial mental or physical abuse as a result of certain crimes (including extortion, blackmail, or severe workplace abuse) and are helpful to law enforcement or government officials in the investigation.
- Protections from deportation are provided during the pending application.

metadata.context: From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.

pageContent:
[From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.]

### T Visas (Victims of Human Trafficking)
- Designed for victims of severe forms of trafficking in persons (including labor trafficking or forced labor).
- In 2026, enforcement against forced labor of H-2A/H-2B workers has been highly prioritized by the DOJ, making T Visas heavily relevant for exploited agricultural and manual laborers.

### Deferred Action for Labor Enforcement
- Workers who are victims of or witnesses to labor rights violations (e.g., wage theft, unsafe conditions) and who report these to agencies like the DOL or NLRB can apply for temporary protection from deportation and work authorization to aid in the investigation.

## 3. Temporary Employment Visas (2026 Context)

### H-1B (Specialty Occupations)
- **2026 Updates:** The H-1B lottery system is now weighted based on wage levels, prioritizing higher-paid roles.
- **Online Presence Review:** The U.S. State Department requires disclosure of social media identifiers for H-1B workers and dependents.

metadata.context: From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.

pageContent:
[From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.]

### H-2A (Temporary Agricultural Workers)
- For seasonal/temporary agricultural labor.
- Employers must provide housing, transportation, and meals (or cooking facilities).
- H-2A workers frequently face labor violations. If an employer confiscates passports or forces labor beyond the contract, the worker may qualify for a T Visa.

### H-2B (Temporary Non-Agricultural Workers)
- For seasonal/temporary non-agricultural work (e.g., landscaping, hospitality, seafood processing).
- Workers are entitled to the prevailing wage and cannot be charged recruitment fees by the employer or their agents.

## 4. Employer Compliance and I-9 Verification

metadata.context: From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.

pageContent:
[From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.]

## 4. Employer Compliance and I-9 Verification

### Form I-9 and E-Verify
- Employers are required to verify the identity and employment authorization of all individuals hired in the U.S. using Form I-9.
- ICE conducts unannounced I-9 inspections. However, during union organizing drives or labor disputes, ICE generally halts enforcement at the worksite to prevent employers from weaponizing immigration enforcement against protesting workers.

### Employment Authorization Documents (EAD)
- **2026 Policy Constraints:** USCIS reduced the validity period for most EAD categories to 18 months, requiring workers to renew earlier to prevent lapses in work authorization.

## 5. State-Level Variations (e.g., California 2026)
- **Workplace Know Your Rights Act (California):** Requires employers to provide annual employee rights notices regarding immigration and allows employees to designate emergency contacts in case of an ICE raid or detention at work. Stricter than federal baselines.

metadata.context: From Immigration & Employment Law — covers worker protections regardless of status, U/T Visas, H-1B/H-2A/H-2B visas, I-9/E-Verify, and ICE enforcement policies.

pageContent:
[From OSHA — covers workplace safety rights, injury reporting, workers compensation, whistleblower protections, and employer safety obligations.]

# OSHA y Compensación de Trabajadores (Workers' Compensation) - 2026

## 1. El OSH Act: Derechos Básicos de los Trabajadores
El Occupational Safety and Health Act (Ley OSH) garantiza a los trabajadores el derecho a:
- Un lugar de trabajo seguro y libre de peligros graves reconocidos.
- Recibir entrenamiento e información sobre químicos y riesgos peligrosos en un idioma que entiendan.
- Acceder a los registros y resultados de inspecciones, así como a sus propios registros médicos laborales.
- Presentar quejas confidenciales ante la OSHA sin temor a represalias o "Wrongful Termination".

## 2. Represalias (Whistleblower Protection)
A un empleador **se le prohíbe rotundamente** despedir, degradar, reducir horas o tomar represalias contra un empleado por ejercer sus derechos de seguridad (p. ej., solicitar una inspección OSHA o quejarse de falta de equipo).

metadata.context: From OSHA — covers workplace safety rights, injury reporting, workers compensation, whistleblower protections, and employer safety obligations.

pageContent:
[From OSHA — covers workplace safety rights, injury reporting, workers compensation, whistleblower protections, and employer safety obligations.]

## 3. Manejo de Lesiones y Accidentes (Workers' Compensation)
Si un accidente laboral ocurre:
- El trabajador debe reportar el accidente inmediatamente (idealmente dentro de 24 horas y, a más tardar, en 30 días según el estado).
- Los gastos iniciales médicos hospitalarios relacionados directamente con la función laboral están amparados bajo seguros mandatorios de Workers' Compensation (excepto en Texas, bajo ciertas condiciones de suscripción).
- **Proceso 2026:** OSHA mandata que los empleadores notifiquen accidentes fatales en menos de 8 horas, y cualquier hospitalización (o pérdida de ojo, amputación) dentro de las 24 horas siguientes. No notificarlo genera multas por hasta de $16,550.

metadata.context: From OSHA — covers workplace safety rights, injury reporting, workers compensation, whistleblower protections, and employer safety obligations.

pageContent:
[From OSHA — covers workplace safety rights, injury reporting, workers compensation, whistleblower protections, and employer safety obligations.]

## 4. Heat Illness Standards 2026 (Nuevo)
- A partir de las regulaciones propuestas y validadas hacia 2026, todos los empleadores de trabajos presenciales y externos deben proveer hidratación pagada, descansos regulados en sombras, y un protocolo de aclimatación para nuevos empleados con el fin de prevenir los golpes de calor. La violación de esta norma lleva multas cuantiosas.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

# Personal Injury & Auto Accident Law in the United States (2026)

This document covers U.S. personal injury law as it applies to motor vehicle accidents, including car crashes, truck accidents, motorcycle collisions, pedestrian injuries, and rideshare incidents. It addresses both general personal injury claims AND work-related vehicle accidents covered under Workers' Compensation.

## 1. Fault vs. No-Fault Insurance Systems

The United States operates under two primary systems for car accident claims:

### Fault (Tort) States
In fault states, the party responsible for causing the accident must pay for damages. The injured party files a claim against the at-fault driver's insurance. Most U.S. states operate under this system, including **Texas, California, Illinois, Georgia, Ohio, and Arizona**.

metadata.context: From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

### No-Fault States (PIP Required)
In no-fault states, each driver's own insurance covers their injuries regardless of who caused the crash. This coverage is called **Personal Injury Protection (PIP)**. The no-fault states as of 2026 are: **Florida, Hawaii, Kansas, Kentucky, Massachusetts, Michigan, Minnesota, New Jersey, New York, North Dakota, Pennsylvania, and Utah**.

- In **Kentucky, New Jersey, and Pennsylvania**, drivers can opt out of no-fault and retain the right to sue.
- **Florida 2026 Update:** Florida is transitioning away from its no-fault PIP system. The full repeal of mandatory PIP takes effect **July 1, 2026**, moving Florida to a bodily injury liability-based system similar to Georgia and Texas.

metadata.context: From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

### When You Can Sue in a No-Fault State
Drivers in no-fault states can file lawsuits only if injuries meet certain severity thresholds:
- **Verbal threshold:** Injuries must be described as serious (e.g., permanent disfigurement, loss of bodily function).
- **Monetary threshold:** Medical bills must exceed a specific dollar amount (e.g., Massachusetts requires damages exceeding $2,000).

## 2. Comparative Negligence Rules

When both parties share blame, state negligence laws determine compensation:

### Pure Comparative Negligence
States like **California, New York, and Florida (pre-2024)** allow you to recover damages even if you are 99% at fault, though your award is reduced by your percentage of fault.

metadata.context: From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

### Pure Comparative Negligence
States like **California, New York, and Florida (pre-2024)** allow you to recover damages even if you are 99% at fault, though your award is reduced by your percentage of fault.

### Modified Comparative Negligence (50% or 51% Bar)
States like **Illinois, Texas, and Florida (post-2024)** bar recovery if you are 50% or 51% or more at fault. Florida adopted modified comparative negligence under HB 837 (signed March 2023), barring injury victims found more than 50% at fault from recovering damages.

### Contributory Negligence
In a few states (**Alabama, Maryland, North Carolina, Virginia, and Washington D.C.**), if you are found even 1% at fault, you recover nothing.

## 3. What to Do After a Car Accident (Step-by-Step)

metadata.context: From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

1. **Ensure safety:** Move to a safe location if possible. Turn on hazard lights.
2. **Call 911:** Report the accident and request police and medical assistance if needed.
3. **Document everything:** Take photos and videos of all vehicles, damage, road conditions, traffic signs, skid marks, and any visible injuries from multiple angles.
4. **Exchange information:** Get the other driver's name, address, phone number, insurance company, policy number, and license plate.
5. **Get witnesses:** Collect names and contact information of any witnesses.
6. **Get the police report:** Ask the responding officer for the report number and how to obtain a copy.
7. **Seek medical attention:** Visit a doctor within 24–72 hours even if you feel fine. Some injuries (whiplash, concussions, internal bleeding) have delayed symptoms.
8. **Report to your insurance:** Notify your insurer within 24–72 hours to avoid claim denial.

metadata.context: From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

8. **Report to your insurance:** Notify your insurer within 24–72 hours to avoid claim denial.
9. **File a DMV report if required:** Many states require reporting to the DMV within 10–30 days if injuries occurred or damage exceeds a threshold (e.g., California requires SR-1 form within 10 days if anyone was injured or damage exceeds $1,000).
10. **Consult a personal injury attorney:** Especially if there are serious injuries, disputed fault, or if the other driver is uninsured.

metadata.context: From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

## 4. Types of Damages (Compensation)

### Economic Damages (Special Damages)
Measurable financial losses:
- **Medical expenses:** Emergency room, hospital stays, surgeries, prescriptions, physical therapy, rehabilitation, future medical care.
- **Lost wages:** Income missed during recovery.
- **Loss of earning capacity:** If injuries permanently reduce your ability to work.
- **Property damage:** Vehicle repair or replacement costs.
- **Out-of-pocket costs:** Transportation to medical appointments, home modifications, assistive devices.

### Non-Economic Damages (General Damages)
Intangible harm:
- **Pain and suffering:** Physical pain and discomfort from injuries.
- **Emotional distress:** Anxiety, depression, PTSD, insomnia resulting from the accident.
- **Loss of enjoyment of life:** Inability to participate in hobbies, activities, or daily life.
- **Loss of consortium:** Impact on relationships with spouse or family.

metadata.context: From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

### Punitive Damages
Awarded to punish the defendant for extreme recklessness (e.g., DUI accidents, road rage). Requires proof of malicious intent or gross negligence. Not available in all states and often capped.

## 5. Statute of Limitations by State (Key Examples)

The statute of limitations sets the deadline for filing a personal injury lawsuit. Missing this deadline forfeits your right to compensation.

| State | Personal Injury | Property Damage |
|-------|----------------|-----------------|
| California | 2 years | 3 years |
| Texas | 2 years | 2 years |
| New York | 3 years | 3 years |
| Florida | 2 years (post-HB 837) | 4 years |
| Illinois | 2 years | 5 years |
| New Jersey | 2 years | 6 years |
| Pennsylvania | 2 years | 2 years |
| Ohio | 2 years | 2 years |
| Georgia | 2 years | 4 years |
| Michigan | 3 years | 3 years |

metadata.context: From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

**Tolling exceptions:** The clock may pause if the injured party is a minor (until they reach age of majority), is mentally incapacitated, or if the defendant leaves the state.

## 6. Work-Related Vehicle Accidents (Workers' Compensation Intersection)

**CRITICAL:** If a car accident happens while working, the injured worker has BOTH Workers' Compensation rights AND potential personal injury claims.

### When Workers' Compensation Covers a Car Accident
- The employee was **driving as part of their job duties** (delivery drivers, truck drivers, salespeople, ride-share drivers on active trips, couriers, field technicians).
- The employee was **running a work errand** for the employer.
- The employee was **traveling between job sites** (not home-to-work commute).

metadata.context: From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

### The "Coming and Going" Rule
Generally, accidents during the **regular commute** (home to work and back) are NOT covered by Workers' Compensation. However, exceptions exist:
- If the employer provides transportation.
- If the employee was running a work errand during the commute.
- If the employee has no fixed workplace (traveling workers).
- If the commute involves a special hazard related to employment.

### Dual Claims (Workers' Comp + Personal Injury)
If a **third party** (not the employer or coworker) caused the accident, the injured worker may file:
1. A **Workers' Compensation claim** for medical bills and lost wages (no-fault, so negligence doesn't matter).
2. A **personal injury lawsuit** against the at-fault third party for full damages (including pain and suffering, which Workers' Comp does not cover).

metadata.context: From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

### Employer Liability
Employers may be directly liable if:
- They **failed to maintain company vehicles**.
- They **pressured employees to drive long hours** without rest.
- They did not provide **proper safety training**.
- They required employees to use **unsafe routes or vehicles**.

## 7. Rideshare Accidents (Uber / Lyft) — 2026

Insurance coverage for rideshare accidents depends on the driver's app status:

### Period 1: App Off
Only the driver's personal auto insurance applies. Uber/Lyft provide no coverage.

### Period 2: App On, Waiting for Ride Request
Uber/Lyft provide contingent liability coverage (only if personal insurance denies the claim):
- **$50,000** per person for bodily injury.
- **$100,000** per accident for bodily injury.
- **$30,000** for property damage.

[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

### Period 3: Ride Accepted or Passenger in Vehicle
Uber/Lyft's **$1 million** third-party liability policy is active. Contingent comprehensive and collision coverage also applies if the driver carries those on their personal policy.

### 2026 Update (California)
California reduced TNC (Transportation Network Company) UM/UIM requirements from $1 million per trip to **$60,000 per person / $300,000 per incident** effective January 1, 2026.

## 8. Uninsured and Underinsured Motorist Claims

If the at-fault driver has no insurance or insufficient coverage:
- **Uninsured Motorist (UM) coverage** on your own policy covers your injuries and damages.
- **Underinsured Motorist (UIM) coverage** fills the gap between the at-fault driver's policy limits and your actual damages.
- Many states require UM/UIM coverage. Check your policy.

## 9. Rights of Undocumented Immigrants in Auto Accidents

metadata.context: From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

## 9. Rights of Undocumented Immigrants in Auto Accidents

**Regardless of immigration status**, all individuals in the United States have the legal right to:
- **File a personal injury claim** if injured by another party's negligence.
- **Seek full compensation** for medical expenses, lost wages, pain and suffering, and property damage.
- **File a police report** without fear of immigration consequences.
- **Access emergency medical treatment** under EMTALA (Emergency Medical Treatment and Labor Act).

### Key Protections
- Filing a personal injury claim **does not trigger deportation proceedings**.
- **ICE and USCIS are barred** from targeting individuals for pursuing legal action.
- Immigration status is generally **inadmissible as evidence** in personal injury cases in many states.
- Courts have consistently held that immigration status does not negate the right to legal redress.

metadata.context: From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.

pageContent:
[From Personal Injury & Auto Accidents — covers fault/no-fault states, PIP, comparative negligence, rideshare liability, statute of limitations, and undocumented immigrant rights in accident cases.]

### If the Accident Was Work-Related
Undocumented workers involved in on-the-job vehicle accidents are fully protected by Workers' Compensation in most states, and by OSHA regulations. Employers cannot retaliate or threaten to report immigration status.

## 10. Common Types of Vehicle Accidents Covered

- **Car-on-car collisions** (rear-end, T-bone, head-on, sideswipe)
- **Truck accidents** (18-wheelers, delivery trucks — may involve federal FMCSA regulations)
- **Motorcycle accidents**
- **Pedestrian hit by vehicle**
- **Bicycle vs. vehicle accidents**
- **Rideshare accidents** (Uber, Lyft — see Section 7)
- **Hit-and-run accidents**
- **DUI/DWI accidents** (potential punitive damages)
- **Road defect / government liability** (potholes, missing signs, construction zones)
- **Defective vehicle parts** (product liability claims against manufacturers)
